request.question: Which Canadian singer sang " something stronger" in the musical applause?

title: Sweden in the Eurovision Song Contest 1965
Applause, Applause
The Singer Sang His Song
Jumbo (Bee Gees song)
Applause (Bonnie Franklin song)
Anna Russell
Spin It Again! Live in Munich
Leland Palmer (actress)
Jennifer Gillis
Len Cariou
sentences: After a year of absence, Sweden competed in the Eurovision Song Contest 1965 with the opera singer Ingvar Wixell, and the song "Absent Friend" (originally "Annorstädes vals") composed by Dag Wirén.
For the first time, one singer sang all the songs in the national final, and Ingvar was also the first male singer to represent Sweden.
It was the first ESC entry that was not performed in the country's native language; this led to the introduction of the language rule the following year.
Applause, Applause was a Canadian musical variety television miniseries which aired on CBC Television in 1974.
"The Singer Sang His Song" is a song written by Barry, Robin and Maurice Gibb and recorded by the English rock group Bee Gees, released in early 1968 as a single along with Jumbo.
In some countries the song was the B-side of Jumbo but in others they were promoted as a double A-side.
"Jumbo" is a song released by the Bee Gees, written by Barry, Robin & Maurice Gibb.
It was released as a double A side with "The Singer Sang His Song" but featured as the lead track in some territories.

"Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.
The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.

The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.
Anna Russell (born Anna Claudia Russell-Brown; 27 December 191118 October 2006) was an English–Canadian singer and comedian.
She gave many concerts in which she sang and played comic musical sketches on the piano.

Among her best-known works are her concert performances and famous recordings of "The Ring of the Nibelungs (An Analysis)" – a humorous 22-minute synopsis of Richard Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen" – and (on the same album) her parody "How to Write Your Own Gilbert and Sullivan Opera."
Spin It Again!
Live in Munich is a live album and video by the Canadian band Saga.
It was recorded in 2012 at the end of their 20/20 tour and celebrates the reunion with original singer Michael Sadler.
It was also the first Saga release featuring their new drummer Mike Thorne.
Besides three songs from "20/20", it includes mainly songs from the first five Saga albums.
The concert was released on different formats by edel records/earMUSIC, including a double CD, DVD (Super Jewel Box packaging) and Blu-Ray.
The video formats also include a twenty-minute tour documentary.
The CD has edited applause between the main set and the encores, but otherwise the performance is identical.
As is typical for Saga shows in Germany, Michael Sadler does some German announcements.
The concert also includes a litte "unplugged" set.
After returning to the stage, Sadler says "What shall I play?"
He starts on "Images (Chapter 1)", then stops, goes into "No Regrets (Chapter 5)", stops again and finally declares "I got it!"
before launching into "Tired World (Chapter 6)".
Leland Palmer (born June 16, 1945, Port Washington, New York) is an American actress, dancer, and singer who has appeared on stage, in motion pictures, and on television.
She appeared on Broadway in "Bajour", "A Joyful Noise", "Hello, Dolly!
", "Applause", and "Pippin".
Palmer received two Tony Award nominations: in 1967 for featured actress in a musical (Miss Jimmie in "A Joyful Noise"), and in 1973 for actress in a musical (Fastrada in "Pippin").
She appeared as Viola in the rock musical "Your Own Thing" during a highly regarded run at the Huntington Hartford Theater in Los Angeles in August 1968.

Jennifer Kristine Gillis (born 26 April 1996) is a Canadian singer, dancer and actress with an extensive resume in musical theatre, television, singing, radio, recording, and animated voice-over work.

Gillis is most noted for performing in Sir Andrew Lloyd Webber's CBC TV reality show "Over the Rainbow"-a competition to be cast as the leading role of Dorothy Gale in Lloyd Webber's forthcoming production of "The Wizard of Oz" in Toronto, Ontario.
Being the youngest aspiring singer in the competition, she singularly represented her province of British Columbia and as a result was named the Top 6th musical theatre performer in all of Canada.
Since "Over the Rainbow", Gillis sang the Canadian national anthem "O Canada" for the Prime Minister of Canada, Stephen Harper on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, Ontario on Canada Day in 2013.
She aspires to become a Broadway actress someday and wants to dedicate her life to performing.
Leonard Joseph "Len" Cariou (born September 30, 1939) is a Canadian actor, best known for his portrayal of Sweeney Todd in the original cast of "", which earned him a Tony award.
He currently plays the patriarch, Henry Reagan, NYPD Police Commissioner (retired), in the multi-generational television series "Blue Bloods" on CBS.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Which Canadian singer sang " something stronger" in the musical applause?
hop1_docs: Applause, Applause | Applause, Applause was a Canadian musical variety television miniseries which aired on CBC Television in 1974.

Applause (Bonnie Franklin song) | "Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.  The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.  The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.

Siti binti Saad | Siti binti Saad (1880–1950) was a pioneering artist in the taraab genre of east African music.  In an era in which male singers predominated, she was a pioneer as a woman singer in the genre: she was the first woman in East Africa to record her music in an album.  In contrast to previous singers who sang in Arabic, she sang in Swahili.  She sang in cities of the coast of Tanganyika and Zanzibar.

Parichay (singer) | Parichay (born August 22) is an Indo-Canadian singer, composer, and music producer.  He has released three albums and collaborated in Bollywood film songs including a remix of "Yamla Pagla Deewana" and "O Meri Chandni" from the film "Chaar Din Ki Chandni" which he sang with Sunidhi Chauhan.  In 2016, he composed music for the Bollywood film "Loveshhuda".

Applause Cheer Boo Hiss | Applause Cheer Boo Hiss is an EP by Canadian indie rock band Land of Talk, released on April 4, 2006 on Dependent Music in Canada.  It would later be released on Rebel Group Records in the United States on March 20, 2007.

Applause (musical) | Applause is a musical with a book by Betty Comden and Adolph Green, lyrics by Lee Adams, and music by Charles Strouse.  The musical is based on the 1950 film "All About Eve" and the short story on which the movie is based, Mary Orr's "The Wisdom of Eve".  The story centers on aging star Margo Channing, who innocently takes a fledgling actress under her wing, unaware that the ruthless Eve is plotting to steal her career and her man.

Stronger (Carlene Carter album) | Stronger is an album released in 2008 by American country music singer Carlene Carter.  Included is "I'm So Cool," a song originally recorded on Carter's 1980 album, "Musical Shapes".
response.summary_1: Evidence: passage [2] states the single's B-side featured Lauren Bacall performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou. Bridge entity: Len Cariou. Missing fact: which Canadian singer sang "Something Stronger" in Applause (and whether the song title is actually "Something Stronger" vs. "Something Greater") is not confirmed in the passages. Retrieval directions: (1) open Len Cariou's biography/credits (e.g., Wikipedia page) to check his Applause credits and any performance of "Something Greater"/"Something Stronger"; (2) open the Applause (musical) or original cast recording / song list page (e.g., Wikipedia "Applause (musical)" or the original cast album) to verify the exact song title and credited singer. Clarification: the passages show "Something Greater" — please confirm if you meant "Something Stronger" or a different song.
response.hop2_query:
Search and open:
1) "Len Cariou Wikipedia" — check his biography/credits for Applause, and any mention of performing a song titled "Something Greater" or "Something Stronger".
2) "Applause (musical) Wikipedia" — open song list/original cast recording page to confirm the exact song titles and which cast member(s) sing "Something Greater"/"Something Stronger".
3) "Applause original cast recording track list" or "Applause cast album Something Greater" — verify track credits and performers (Lauren Bacall, Len Cariou).
4) "Lauren Bacall Something Greater Len Cariou" — confirm the B-side/single information and credited performers.
If possible, report the precise song title used in the musical and name the Canadian performer credited for singing that song (and note if the correct performer is Len Cariou).
hop2_docs: Applause (Bonnie Franklin song) | "Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.  The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.  The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.

Len Cariou | Leonard Joseph "Len" Cariou (born September 30, 1939) is a Canadian actor, best known for his portrayal of Sweeney Todd in the original cast of "", which earned him a Tony award.  He currently plays the patriarch, Henry Reagan, NYPD Police Commissioner (retired), in the multi-generational television series "Blue Bloods" on CBS.

Love or Something Like It (song) | "Love or Something Like It" is a song co-written and performed by American country music artist Kenny Rogers.  It was released in May 1978 as the first single and title track from the album "Love or Something Like It".  The song was written by Rogers and Steven Glassmeyer (a member of Rogers's backing band) and was Kenny Rogers's third number one on the country chart.  The single stayed at number one for one week and spent a total of ten weeks on the country chart.

Something in Red (song) | "Something in Red" is a song written by Angela Kaset and recorded by American country music artist Lorrie Morgan.  It was released in March 1992 as the fourth single and title track from the album "Something in Red".  The song reached number 14 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart and peaked at number 10 on the "RPM" Country Tracks in Canada.

You're Something Special to Me | "You're Something Special to Me" the title of a song written by David Anthony, and recorded by American country music artist George Strait.  It was released in December 1985 as the second and final single from his album "Something Special".  The song peaked at #4 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks and the Canadian "RPM" charts.

You've Got to Stand for Something (song) | "You've Got to Stand for Something" is a song co-written and recorded by American country music singer Aaron Tippin.  It was released in October 1990 as his debut single and the title track to his album "You've Got to Stand for Something".  It reached the top ten on the country singles chart in early 1991.  Tippin wrote the song with Buddy Brock.

Something or Nothing | "Something or Nothing" is a song by British rock band Uriah Heep.  The song was written by Ken Hensley, Mick Box and Gary Thain and sung by David Byron.  The song is the sixth track on their seventh album "Wonderworld", it is also the first track on the second side of the album.  "Something or Nothing" was recorded in Munich, Germany, in a studio called "Musicland" during January and March 1974.  The B-side of the song is "What Can I Do" which has never been released on the album "Wonderworld".  The song is being played with only four chords: D,A,C and G.  The song has been performed during Uriah Heep live concerts, and was included on their second live album "Live at Shepperton '74" as the fourth track.
hop2_titles: You've Got to Stand for Something (song)
response.summary_2: The song is titled "Something Greater" (not "Something Stronger"), and the Canadian performer who sang it in Applause was Len Cariou, who performed the piece with Lauren Bacall on the single's B-side.
